old_claim.note: Notarial Records provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped claim assesses losses to insured houses and buildings, so property ownership supplies the claim subject without being a specialized adjustment mechanism.

source_shape.source_locator: The London Museum explains that the Great Fire destroyed thousands of properties and that Barbon's Fire Office insured London houses by 1680.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames early fire insurance around insured houses and property losses after the Great Fire.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Insured property is the object being assessed in the scoped claims practice. Medieval notarial registers are a different record institution and are not required for this London fire-insurance scope.

ontology_before: Insurance Claims Adjustment depended on medieval notarial records, laundering a broad legal-document dependency into a London fire-insurance claims practice.
ontology_after: Early Insurance Claims Assessment depends contextually on property concepts because the claims are for insured houses and buildings, without treating notaries as a direct enabling technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: insurance_claims_adjustment now depends on property_concepts and no longer directly depends on notarial_records.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to notarial maritime protest or notarial loss-recording practice.
A source showed London fire-insurance claims assessment depended directly on medieval-style notarial registers.
The property_concepts node were later narrowed so it no longer covers owned buildings or insured property.
short_reason: The claim subject is insured property, not notarial record production.
